confidence.disclosure: The Prophet's life is the most documented in early Islamic history, anchored in Ibn Ishaq's sirah and the hadith collections. The Medinan decade (622-632) is attested at month-level precision from multiple independent early sources. Childhood and pre-prophethood entries (marked 'year' or 'period') draw on later traditional reports and should be read as traditional rather than strictly documented. Sources were compiled 100-200 years after his death.

journey.event: Born in the Year of the Elephant into the Banu Hashim clan of the Quraysh tribe. His father Abdullah had died before his birth.
Sent to be nursed and raised in the desert by Halimah al-Sa'diyya, following the Quraysh custom of sending infants to Bedouin foster families for a healthier upbringing.
His mother Aminah bint Wahb died on the return journey from visiting relatives in Medina. He was orphaned at the age of six.
His grandfather and guardian Abd al-Muttalib died. Care passed to his uncle Abu Talib, chief of Banu Hashim.
Travelled with his uncle Abu Talib on a trading caravan to Syria. The monk Bahira reportedly recognised signs of prophethood in the young boy.

Married Khadijah bint Khuwaylid, a successful merchant widow fifteen years his senior. She had employed him to lead her trading caravan to Syria and proposed marriage after being impressed by his character.
Arbitrated a dispute among Quraysh clans over who should place the Black Stone during the rebuilding of the Ka'bah. He placed it on a cloth and had representatives of each clan carry it together.
Received the first revelation from the Angel Jibril during a retreat in the Cave of Hira. The first verses revealed were the opening of Surah Al-Alaq: 'Read in the name of your Lord who created.'
Began public preaching, calling the Quraysh from Mount Safa. Most of the tribal leaders rejected the message, but a small community of believers began to form.
Persecution of Muslims by the Quraysh intensified. He instructed a group of followers to emigrate to Abyssinia (Ethiopia) to seek protection under the just Christian king, the Negus.
The Quraysh imposed a total boycott on Banu Hashim and Banu al-Muttalib. The clans were confined to a narrow valley, cut off from trade and social contact for approximately three years.
The Year of Sorrow. His wife Khadijah and his uncle Abu Talib both died within weeks of each other, removing his closest emotional support and his clan's political protection.
Travelled to Ta'if to seek support from the Thaqif tribe. He was rejected and stoned by the townspeople. He prayed for their guidance rather than their destruction.

The Night Journey (Isra) and Ascension (Mi'raj). Transported from the Sacred Mosque in Mecca to Al-Aqsa in Jerusalem, then ascended through the heavens. The five daily prayers were prescribed during this journey.
The First Pledge of Aqaba. Twelve men from Yathrib (Medina) pledged allegiance, accepting Islam and promising to uphold its moral principles.
The Second Pledge of Aqaba. Seventy-five people from Yathrib pledged to protect him as they would their own families, paving the way for the migration.

Departed Mecca secretly with Abu Bakr. They hid in the Cave of Thawr for three days while Quraysh search parties scoured the area. A spider's web and a dove's nest at the cave entrance are reported to have concealed them.
Arrived at Quba after travelling north along the coastal route. Established the first mosque in Islam, Masjid Quba, during a stay of several days.
Entered Medina to a joyful reception. His camel knelt at the site where the Prophet's Mosque (Masjid al-Nabawi) would be built. Established the Constitution of Medina, a multi-faith social contract.
The Battle of Badr. A force of 313 Muslims defeated a Quraysh army of nearly 1,000. This decisive victory established the Muslim community as a political and military force in Arabia.
The Battle of Uhud. The Muslim force suffered a setback when archers abandoned their positions. The Prophet was injured when struck by stones. Hamza ibn Abd al-Muttalib was killed.

The Battle of the Trench (Khandaq). On the advice of Salman al-Farisi, a large trench was dug around Medina's exposed northern side. A coalition of 10,000 besieged the city for nearly a month but failed to breach the defences.

The Treaty of Hudaybiyyah. A ten-year truce was signed with the Quraysh. Though initially seen as unfavourable, the Quran called it a 'clear victory' (48:1). It allowed peaceful propagation of Islam across Arabia.
The Conquest of Khaybar. The fortified oasis stronghold north of Medina was taken after a prolonged siege. Ali ibn Abi Talib led the final assault.
The Lesser Pilgrimage (Umrat al-Qada). Under the terms of Hudaybiyyah, the Muslims entered Mecca peacefully for three days to perform Umrah — their first return since the Hijra.

The Battle of Mu'tah. An expedition of 3,000 Muslims faced a large Byzantine-allied force. Zayd ibn Harithah, Ja'far ibn Abi Talib, and Abdullah ibn Rawahah were killed. Khalid ibn al-Walid took command and withdrew the army safely.

The Conquest of Mecca (Fath Makkah). An army of 10,000 entered the city with almost no resistance. The Prophet granted a general amnesty and cleansed the Ka'bah of its 360 idols, declaring 'Truth has come and falsehood has vanished.'
The Battle of Hunayn. Immediately after the conquest, the Hawazin and Thaqif tribes attacked. The Muslims were initially ambushed in a narrow valley but rallied and won decisively.

The Siege of Ta'if. After Hunayn, the Muslim army besieged the fortified city of Ta'if for about twenty days. The siege was lifted without a decisive outcome; the Thaqif later voluntarily accepted Islam.

The Expedition to Tabuk. The largest military expedition, with 30,000 men marching north toward the Byzantine frontier. No battle occurred, but several northern tribes submitted. It demonstrated Muslim reach to the edge of the Byzantine Empire.

The Farewell Pilgrimage (Hajjat al-Wada'). Delivered the Farewell Sermon at Arafat before approximately 100,000 pilgrims, declaring the equality of all people, the sanctity of life and property, and the completion of the religion.
Died in the arms of Aisha in her chamber adjoining the Prophet's Mosque. His last words included counsel to uphold prayer and treat dependants well. He was buried where he passed away.
journey.source: Sahih al-Bukhari; Sahih Muslim; Ibn Ishaq
